__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_16__: Everything a first-time or experienced buyer needs to evaluate, price, and close a SaaS acquisition — from the initial search through post-close integration.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_17__: Buying a SaaS business is the fastest path to recurring revenue ownership — but it's also the easiest way to overpay for a shrinking asset. This guide covers the complete acquisition lifecycle from a buyer's perspective, with specific attention to the diligence signals that separate good deals from bad ones.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_18__: 1. Sourcing Deals: Where to Find SaaS Businesses for Sale
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_20__: Acquire.com, Flippa, MicroAcquire, and Quiet Light Brokerage list SaaS businesses from $5K–$10M+ asking prices. Each has different quality filters — Acquire.com tends to have higher-quality listings with verified data rooms.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_22__: SaaS businesses not for sale publicly are often available at the right price. Look for solo-founder businesses, retiring boomer SaaS owners, or lifestyle businesses whose founders have moved on to other projects. Signals: stale blogs, no new features in 12+ months, founder posting on LinkedIn about their "next venture."
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_24__: FE International, Dealflow, and Quiet Light Brokerage manage curated deal flows. Their fees (10–15% of deal value) are worth it for first-time buyers who need handholding through the process.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_26__: Before spending hours on diligence, filter deals with three data points you can get from public info or a 5-minute seller call:
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_27__: MRR > $3K for micro-SaaS or > $20K for growth-stage deals
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_28__: Revenue not concentrated in a single customer

__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_41__: 4. Valuation: What Multiple Should You Pay?
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_42__: In 2026, SaaS valuation multiples have compressed from the 2021 peak. Current ranges by deal size:
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_44__: 2–3.5× ARR. These are often founder-dependent. Price in transition risk.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_46__: 3–5× ARR. Wide range depends on growth rate and churn.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_48__: 4–7× ARR. NRR above 100% commands the premium.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_50__: 5–10× ARR. Institutional buyers compete here; data quality and team depth matter.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_52__: Don't guess on multiples. Upload the target's CSV and Churn Lens computes churn-adjusted ARR, quality-score-adjusted multiple, and a recommended offer range.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_54__: 5. Deal Structure: Earn-outs, Holdbacks, and Transition Periods
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_55__: Most SaaS acquisitions include a transition period where the seller stays on as a contractor (30–90 days for micro-SaaS, 6–12 months for larger deals). Structure notes:
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_57__: Tie 10–20% of the purchase price to top-customer retention at 12 months post-close
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_59__: Hold back 5–10% for 6 months to cover billing disputes or unexpected churn
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_61__: The seller must respond within 24 hours during the transition period
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_63__: The most common failure point is integration that tries to change too much too fast. The first 90 days should be:
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_65__: Observe. Don't change pricing, don't redesign the product, don't fire anyone. Map all systems, processes, and vendor relationships.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_67__: Stabilize. Fix any broken payment flows, update the billing system, and ensure support channels are operational with your team.
__TR_buying-a-saas-business-guide_69__: Optimize. Start with the highest-impact change — often pricing or packaging — which the seller under-optimized.
